text: The surgeon begins creating a flap by dissecting retrorectus medial to the semilunar line. The epigastric vessels are inadvertently dropped and then carefully swept back into position.

The surgeon performs a limited release of the transversus abdominis muscle above the arcuate line to access the preperitoneal plane. The arcuate line is visible at the level of the Spiegelian hernia. A volcano sign is created, and the surgeon begins reducing the hernia contents.

The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal space lateral to the hernia defect, carefully dissecting fat from the indirect ring while identifying cord structures to avoid injury. The surgeon releases fat from the peritoneum to create space for a wide mesh overlap, planning to use a 15x15 ProGrip mesh. Medial dissection is performed in the myopectineal orifice to ensure proper placement along the cord.

The surgeon targets the myopectineal orifice (MPO) in the recurrent indirect hernia, identifying the vas deferens and cord structures near the internal ring. The lipoma is visible, possibly contributing to the patient's symptoms. The surgeon positions the mesh, ensuring adequate coverage without a ruler, and proceeds to close the defect using twelve-inch zero V-lock sutures.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh in the preperitoneal space, ensuring proper placement before closing the flap.
